tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 task, the benchmark outcome is correct recognition and documentation of trigeminal neuralgia as the working diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "trigeminal neuralgia" and explains it in patient-friendly language.

Elicits key diagnostic features: recurrent brief (seconds to minutes) unilateral facial pain in trigeminal distribution, severe electric shock/stabbing quality, triggered by touch/chewing/brushing teeth/talking, with pain-free intervals.

Checks for red flags/secondary causes and documents absence/presence: sensory loss or numbness, persistent pain between attacks, bilateral symptoms, age <40, abnormal neuro exam, hearing changes/vertigo, constitutional symptoms, history of malignancy, immunosuppression, or recent trauma/infection.
Performs/requests a focused neurologic and cranial nerve assessment (at least by history if exam not possible) and notes whether facial sensation is intact.
Provides a clear initial treatment explanation that includes "carbamazepine" as first-line therapy and sets expectations for titration and response.

Completes required safety checks before any prescribing discussion becomes a prescription: asks about medication allergies (especially to carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine/other anticonvulsants), checks contraindications (pregnancy, hepatic disease, prior bone marrow suppression, history of serious cutaneous adverse reactions), and checks for major drug interactions (e.g., warfarin, hormonal contraception, macrolides/azole antifungals, other enzyme-inducing drugs).

Counsels on key adverse effects and safety: dizziness/somnolence, nausea, hyponatremia, rash/Stevens-Johnson syndrome warning and to stop/seek care for rash, fever, mouth sores; advises avoiding driving if sedated.

Recommends appropriate follow-up and escalation: reassess within days to weeks for symptom control and side effects; urgent evaluation if new neurologic deficits, atypical features, or severe adverse drug reaction symptoms.

Avoids inappropriate/unsafe recommendations: does not label as dental infection/sinusitis without supporting features; does not recommend opioids as primary therapy for classic trigeminal neuralgia; does not prescribe without performing the required safety checks.
